hn_load_data_d27faeb8c2d6.code:
# Download alpaca data for sft
wget -O data/sft/alpaca_data.json https://github.com/tatsu-lab/stanford_alpaca/raw/main/alpaca_data.json

---

# MMLU dataset
wget -O data/eval/mmlu_data.tar https://people.eecs.berkeley.edu/~hendrycks/data.tar
mkdir -p data/eval/mmlu_data
tar -xvf data/eval/mmlu_data.tar -C data/eval/mmlu_data
mv data/eval/mmlu_data/data data/eval/mmlu && rm -r data/eval/mmlu_data data/eval/mmlu_data.tar

---

# TruthfulQA dataset, open-ended and multiple-choice versions
mkdir -p data/eval/truthfulqa
wget -O data/eval/truthfulqa/truthfulqa.csv https://raw.githubusercontent.com/sylinrl/TruthfulQA/main/data/v0/TruthfulQA.csv
wget -O data/eval/truthfulqa/truthfulqa_mc.json https://raw.githubusercontent.com/sylinrl/TruthfulQA/main/data/mc_task.json
hn_load_data_d27faeb8c2d6.documentation: Downloads and preprocesses benchmark datasets (Alpaca, MMLU, TruthfulQA) for supervised fine-tuning and evaluation. Used in the initial data preparation phase before training or evaluation pipelines begin.

hn_train,_typically_uses,_knowledge_dist.documentation: Applies knowledge distillation during training, where a smaller or pruned student model learns from a larger teacher model. The distillation is activated at epoch 3, typically to stabilize adaptation after initial parameter tuning.

hn_eval,_evaluated_by,_task_performance_.documentation: Evaluates model performance on a downstream NLP task (MNLI) using standard accuracy metrics, which is the primary measure of task performance after fine-tuning and compression.

hn_eval,_evaluated_by,_inference_speed_6.documentation: Inference speed is indirectly measured via evaluation batch size; larger batches improve throughput and are used to benchmark latency and efficiency gains from pruning and distillation.

hn_train,_typically_uses,_adaptive_pruni.documentation: Implements adaptive pruning by dynamically selecting which heads or neurons to prune based on gradient-based salience scores, with pruning triggered at a specific training step (steppoint) to avoid early degradation.

hn_train,_typically_uses,_gradient_based.documentation: Uses gradient-based importance scoring (L2 norm of gradients) to identify least salient parameters for pruning, a common method for adaptive pruning that correlates parameter importance with training signal strength.

hn_train,_typically_uses,_dynamic_parame.documentation: Applies dynamic parameter masking during training via a pruning scheduler that updates masks based on salience scores, enabling iterative pruning without reinitializing weights.

hn_train,_typically_uses,_low_rank_adapt.documentation: Implements Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) by injecting trainable low-rank matrices into attention weights, allowing efficient fine-tuning without modifying the base model parameters. Used to reduce memory and compute overhead while retaining performance.

hn_train,_typically_uses,_pruning_22bb0b.documentation: Applies structured pruning during training using a specified pruner (e.g., Fisher) and MAC constraint to reduce model size. Pruning is scheduled to start at a given step and applied over a set number of batches, enabling dynamic sparsity control.

hn_pruning,_typically_uses,_fisher_infor.documentation: Uses Fisher information-based pruning by computing gradient-based salience scores (L2 norm of gradients) for attention heads and feed-forward neurons. This identifies and removes least important parameters based on their impact on loss.

hn_train,_typically_uses,_knowledge_dist.documentation: Applies knowledge distillation during training, where a smaller or pruned model learns from a full teacher model. Distillation is activated at a specified epoch to stabilize learning after initial adaptation.

hn_eval,_evaluated_by,_task_performance_.documentation: Evaluates model performance on a downstream task (MNLI) using standard accuracy metrics. Task performance is the primary evaluation metric for fine-tuned models in NLP.

hn_eval,_evaluated_by,_memory_footprint_.documentation: Memory footprint is influenced by the combination of LoRA adapter rank and MAC constraint, which together determine the number of trainable parameters and model size. Lower values reduce memory usage during training and inference.

hn_eval,_step_1,_load_eval_dataset_8a936.documentation: Evaluates a model on a downstream task (MNLI) by loading the validation dataset using standardized command-line arguments. Used after training to measure task performance without retraining.

hn_eval,_evaluated_by,_task_performance_.documentation: Evaluates model performance on the MNLI task, a standard natural language inference benchmark. Used to measure accuracy or F1 score after pruning or fine-tuning to assess task retention.

hn_eval,_evaluated_by,_inference_latency.documentation: Performs evaluation to measure inference latency on MNLI, typically inferred from batch processing time under fixed batch size and sequence length. Used to assess deployment efficiency after model compression.

hn_eval,_evaluated_by,_memory_footprint_.documentation: Evaluates memory footprint during inference on MNLI, implied by batch size and model configuration. Used to quantify memory efficiency gains from LoRA and pruning.

hn_eval,_evaluated_by,_task_accuracy_17a.documentation: Measures task accuracy on MNLI during evaluation, the primary metric for assessing whether compression techniques preserve model performance. Used to validate effectiveness of pruning and parameter-efficient tuning.

hn_train,_requires,_pretrained_model_ab1.documentation: Uses a pretrained RoBERTa model as the base for applying LoRA and pruning. Required to initialize the model with learned representations before applying parameter-efficient adaptation.

hn_train,_typically_uses,_parameter_effi.documentation: Applies LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) to fine-tune the model with minimal parameters. Used to reduce memory and compute costs while preserving performance on downstream tasks like MNLI.

hn_train,_typically_uses,_low_rank_adapt.documentation: Implements Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) via lora_r=8 and lora_alpha=16, decomposing weight updates into low-rank matrices. Used to enable efficient fine-tuning without modifying original model weights.

hn_train,_has_config,_learning_rate:1e-5.documentation: Uses a learning rate in the range 1e-5 to 5e-5, typical for fine-tuning transformer models with parameter-efficient methods. Ensures stable convergence when updating low-rank adapters.

hn_train,_has_config,_batch_size:8_to_64.documentation: Sets batch size to 32, within the typical range of 8–64 for transformer fine-tuning. Balances memory usage and gradient stability during training with LoRA and pruning.

hn_train,_has_config,_max_sequence_lengt.documentation: Uses max_seq_length=128, which is below the typical 512–1024 range but common for MNLI due to its sentence-pair nature. Ensures compatibility with model input limits and memory constraints.

hn_train,_typically_uses,_adaptive_pruni.documentation: Implements adaptive pruning via pruning_frequency and pruning_batches, dynamically removing weights during training based on salience. Used to maintain model performance under MAC constraints.

hn_train,_has_config,_pruning_frequency:.documentation: Configures pruning frequency as a fraction (e.g., 0.1), determining how often pruning is applied during training. Used to gradually reduce model size without disrupting convergence.

hn_train,_has_config,_pruning_ratio:0.1_.documentation: Imposes a MAC constraint (e.g., 0.6) to indirectly control pruning ratio, targeting 10–90% sparsity. Used to balance model size reduction and task performance under hardware limits.

hn_train,_typically_uses,_structured_pru.documentation: Applies structured pruning (e.g., neuron or head removal) under MAC constraint to reduce inference latency. Used in conjunction with LoRA to maintain efficiency without unstructured sparsity.

hn_eval,_step_1,_evaluate_task_performan.documentation: Evaluates task performance on MNLI using the trained model. Standard step after training to report accuracy or F1 score, ensuring compression did not degrade downstream performance.

hn_eval,_evaluated_by,_inference_speed_c.documentation: Measures inference speed indirectly via batch processing time and fixed batch size. Used to validate that pruning and LoRA reduce latency without sacrificing accuracy.

hn_eval,_evaluated_by,_model_size_322fa1.documentation: Infers model size reduction from LoRA (low-rank adapters) and pruning under MAC constraint. Used to quantify compression efficiency relative to full fine-tuning.

hn_eval,_evaluated_by,_compression_ratio.documentation: Computes compression ratio by comparing parameter count of pruned LoRA model to original. Used to evaluate efficiency of hybrid compression (LoRA + pruning).

hn_eval,_evaluated_by,_accuracy_drop_6cf.documentation: Measures accuracy drop relative to full fine-tuning baseline. Critical metric to validate that pruning and LoRA maintain performance under aggressive compression.

hn_eval,_evaluated_by,_parameter_count_d.documentation: Reports final parameter count of the pruned LoRA model. Used to quantify parameter efficiency and compare against baseline models.

hn_train,_has_config,_rank_dimension:4_t.documentation: Sets LoRA rank (lora_r=8) within the typical range of 4–64. Controls the expressiveness of low-rank adapters and trades off between compression and performance.

hn_train,_typically_uses,_adapter_based_.documentation: Uses LoRA as an adapter-based architecture, injecting low-rank matrices into attention layers. Enables efficient multi-task learning without modifying base weights.

hn_train,_typically_uses,_dynamic_parame.documentation: Implements dynamic parameter masking via iterative pruning at specified frequency. Masks weights during training to adaptively reduce model size under MAC constraint.

hn_train,_typically_uses,_iterative_prun.documentation: Applies pruning iteratively during training at intervals defined by pruning_frequency. Used to gradually increase sparsity while allowing model recovery via fine-tuning.

hn_train,_typically_uses,_retraining_aft.documentation: Retrains the model after each pruning step by continuing optimization with LoRA. Ensures that pruned weights do not permanently degrade performance.

hn_train,_typically_uses,_quantization_a.documentation: Not explicitly implemented — no quantization flags (e.g., quantization_bits) are present. This pattern is not active in this script.

hn_train,_typically_uses,_knowledge_dist.documentation: No distillation flags (e.g., distillation_weight, teacher_model) are present. Knowledge distillation is not applied in this script.

hn_eval,_step_1,_load_eval_dataset_52c27.documentation: Loads the evaluation dataset for the MMLU benchmark using a specified data directory and number of few-shot examples (ntrain). Used to prepare evaluation data for task performance assessment on multiple-choice questions.

hn_eval,_step_2,_run_inference_cfb87a6b2.documentation: Performs inference on the MMLU evaluation dataset using a pretrained LLaMA model. The script processes inputs in batches and generates predictions for multiple-choice questions, typically invoked during evaluation pipelines.

hn_eval,_step_3,_compute_task_specific_m.documentation: Computes task-specific performance metrics (e.g., accuracy) on the MMLU benchmark by comparing model predictions against ground truth labels. The script outputs results to a specified directory, implying metric calculation is handled internally by run_eval_llama_mmlu.py.

hn_eval,_evaluated_by,_task_accuracy_3cf.documentation: Evaluates task accuracy on the MMLU benchmark, a standard measure for multi-choice question answering. Accuracy is computed as the percentage of correctly answered questions across subjects.

hn_eval,_evaluated_by,_inference_latency.documentation: Measures inference latency during evaluation on the MMLU dataset, inferred from batched inference and timing logs. Latency is typically derived from the time taken to process all evaluation examples, often reported per sample or per batch.

hn_eval,_evaluated_by,_memory_footprint_.documentation: Measures memory footprint during evaluation, inferred from model loading and batch processing. The script uses a fixed batch size and model path, implying memory usage is tracked implicitly during inference on GPU/TPU.

hn_eval,_evaluated_by,_inference_speed_8.documentation: Measures inference speed (e.g., samples per second) during MMLU evaluation. Speed is derived from total evaluation time and number of examples, implied by batched inference and logging.

hn_eval,_evaluated_by,_inference_through.documentation: Measures inference throughput (e.g., tokens or samples processed per second) during MMLU evaluation. Throughput is inferred from eval_batch_size and total evaluation time, commonly reported in model efficiency benchmarks.

hn_eval,_evaluated_by,_inference_memory_.documentation: Measures memory usage during inference on the MMLU benchmark. Memory footprint is tracked implicitly via GPU memory allocation during model loading and batched prediction.

hn_eval,_evaluated_by,_model_size_e85581.documentation: Infers model size from the specified model_name_or_path (e.g., Llama-2-7B), which corresponds to a known parameter count. Model size is not computed in this script but is a key metric reported alongside evaluation results.

hn_eval,_evaluated_by,_parameter_count_0.documentation: Infers parameter count from the model name (e.g., Llama-2-7B implies ~7B parameters). While not computed in this script, parameter count is a standard metric reported in evaluation outputs for compression and efficiency analysis.

hn_eval,_evaluated_by,_task_performance_.documentation: Evaluates overall task performance on the MMLU benchmark using accuracy as the primary metric. This pattern is used to assess how well a model generalizes across diverse knowledge domains in a zero-shot or few-shot setting.

hn_eval,_evaluated_by,_perplexity_c203f5.documentation: Computes perplexity as a language modeling metric during evaluation. Although MMLU is a multiple-choice task, perplexity may be computed internally for each answer option to determine the most likely choice.

hn_eval,_evaluated_by,_f1_score_e096d54b.documentation: Computes F1 score for multi-class classification performance on MMLU. While accuracy is primary, F1 may be computed per subject or aggregated for balanced evaluation across imbalanced categories.

hn_eval,_evaluated_by,_rouge_score_9ea33.documentation: Computes ROUGE score for text generation quality. While MMLU is multiple-choice, ROUGE may be used if the model generates explanations or if the evaluation script supports open-ended answer formats.

hn_eval,_evaluated_by,_lm_eval_harness_m.documentation: Uses the lm-eval-harness framework to compute standardized metrics on MMLU. The script is likely a wrapper around lm-eval-harness, which supports zero-shot/few-shot evaluation with consistent metric reporting.

hn_eval,_requires,_ground_truth_labels_6.documentation: Requires ground truth labels for MMLU evaluation, loaded from the data_dir. Labels are stored in structured format (e.g., JSON or CSV) and matched with model predictions to compute accuracy and other metrics.

hn_eval,_requires,_model_predictions_ab7.documentation: Requires model predictions generated during inference on MMLU. Predictions are typically stored as predicted answer choices (A, B, C, D) and compared against ground truth labels for metric computation.

hn_eval,_step_1,_load_validation_data_18.documentation: Loads the MMLU validation dataset from a specified directory, using few-shot examples (ntrain=5) for context. This is a standard step in evaluation pipelines to prepare input prompts for the model.

hn_eval,_step_2,_generate_model_predicti.documentation: Generates model predictions on the MMLU evaluation set using batched inference. The script tokenizes prompts, runs forward passes, and selects the most likely answer option based on log probabilities.

hn_eval,_evaluated_by,_accuracy_c60ea51f.documentation: Computes overall accuracy on the MMLU benchmark, defined as the percentage of correctly answered multiple-choice questions. This is the primary evaluation metric for this script.

hn_eval,_evaluated_by,_inference_speedup.documentation: Measures inference speedup relative to a baseline (e.g., full fine-tuned model). Speedup is inferred by comparing evaluation time to a reference model, often reported in papers for efficiency claims.

hn_eval,_evaluated_by,_inference_memory_.documentation: Measures memory efficiency during inference relative to a baseline model. Efficiency is computed as the ratio of memory usage between compressed and full models, often reported for quantized or pruned models.

hn_eval,_evaluated_by,_compression_ratio.documentation: Computes compression ratio as the ratio of original model size to compressed model size. Inferred from model_name_or_path (e.g., 7B vs. pruned 3B), though not computed in this script, it is a key metric reported in evaluation outputs.

hn_eval,_evaluated_by,_accuracy_drop_3ad.documentation: Computes accuracy drop relative to a full fine-tuned baseline. This metric quantifies performance degradation due to compression techniques like pruning or quantization, inferred by comparing results to a reference run.

hn_eval,_evaluated_by,_parameter_efficie.documentation: Measures parameter efficiency as task accuracy per million parameters. Used to compare compressed models fairly, especially when model sizes vary due to pruning or adapter methods.

hn_eval,_evaluated_by,_training_memory_u.documentation: Measures memory usage during evaluation, which may include caching activations or storing intermediate results. Though primarily an inference script, memory footprint is often reported for resource-constrained deployment.

hn_eval,_evaluated_by,_training_time_4bf.documentation: Measures total evaluation time (inference time) for reporting training-time efficiency. Though this is an evaluation script, time-to-evaluate is often used as a proxy for training efficiency in compressed models.

hn_eval,_evaluated_by,_inference_time_ce.documentation: Measures total time taken to run inference on the entire MMLU evaluation set. Used to compare latency across models and is often reported in milliseconds per sample or total seconds.

hn_eval,_evaluated_by,_training_memory_e.documentation: Measures memory efficiency during evaluation as a proxy for training efficiency. Lower memory usage during eval implies better compression and potential for efficient training.

hn_eval,_evaluated_by,_training_time_to_.documentation: Measures time-to-accuracy during evaluation as a proxy for training efficiency. Though this script only evaluates, it reports accuracy achieved after training, allowing inference of training efficiency relative to baseline.

hn_eval,_evaluated_by,_latency_ratio_507.documentation: Computes latency ratio between compressed and baseline models. This metric quantifies speedup from pruning/quantization, inferred by comparing evaluation time to a reference model.

hn_eval,_evaluated_by,_training_time_rat.documentation: Computes training time ratio between compressed and baseline models. Though this script evaluates, it assumes training was done previously and reports efficiency gains relative to full fine-tuning.

hn_eval,_evaluated_by,_performance_reten.documentation: Measures performance retention as the ratio of compressed model accuracy to baseline accuracy. A key metric for evaluating whether compression techniques preserve task performance.

hn_eval,_evaluated_by,_task_specific_acc.documentation: Computes accuracy per MMLU subject (e.g., biology, law, history) to analyze domain-specific performance. Used to identify strengths/weaknesses of compressed models across knowledge areas.

hn_eval,_evaluated_by,_bleu_ece09293d816.documentation: Computes BLEU score if the evaluation includes generated text responses. Though MMLU is multiple-choice, some variants may require generation, and BLEU is a standard metric for such cases.

hn_eval,_evaluated_by,_pearson_correlati.documentation: Computes Pearson correlation between model confidence scores and human ratings, if available. Used in tasks with continuous scoring or when evaluating calibration quality.

hn_train,_has_config,_batch_size:8_to_64.documentation: Configures batch size for training and evaluation during model fine-tuning. Typically set between 8 and 64 to balance memory usage and training stability. This script uses 32, which falls within the recommended range.

hn_train,_has_config,_learning_rate:1e-5.documentation: Indicates the use of Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA), a parameter-efficient fine-tuning method that typically employs learning rates in the range of 1e-5 to 5e-4. Although the learning rate value is not explicitly set here, --apply_lora implies its use within the underlying training script, which defaults to this range.

hn_eval,_evaluated_by,_task_performance_.documentation: Evaluates model performance on a downstream task (MNLI in this case). The --do_eval flag triggers evaluation, and --task_name specifies the benchmark, aligning with the pattern of assessing task performance via standard NLP benchmarks.

hn_train,_requires,_pretrained_model_043.documentation: Loads a pretrained model (e.g., roberta-base) as the starting point for fine-tuning. This is a required step in nearly all modern NLP training pipelines to leverage transfer learning.

hn_eval,_evaluated_by,_inference_speed_9.documentation: The test_mode and mask_mode flags suggest evaluation of inference characteristics under different pruning or masking strategies. While not directly measuring speed, this setup is used to infer speed impacts of pruning, aligning with inference speed evaluation patterns.

hn_train,_typically_uses,_fisher_informa.documentation: Applies Fisher information-based pruning during training to identify and remove less important parameters based on second-order gradient statistics. Used when aiming for structured, gradient-aware pruning that preserves task performance with minimal accuracy drop.

hn_train,_has_config,_learning_rate:1e-5.documentation: Sets the learning rate for optimizer during fine-tuning, typically in the range 1e-5 to 1e-4 for parameter-efficient methods like LoRA to avoid catastrophic forgetting while enabling stable adaptation.

hn_eval,_evaluated_by,_inference_latency.documentation: Evaluation phase implicitly measures inference latency by running forward passes on validation data with configured batch size, providing real-world performance estimates for deployed models.

hn_eval,_evaluated_by,_memory_footprint_.documentation: Memory footprint is indirectly measured via batch size and gradient accumulation settings, which determine peak GPU memory usage during training — critical for resource-constrained environments.

hn_train,_typically_uses,_adaptive_tunin.documentation: LoRA enables adaptive tuning by allowing only low-rank adapter weights to be updated during training, while keeping the base model frozen — a core mechanism for efficient and stable adaptation.

hn_parameter_efficient_finetuning,_typic.documentation: Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) is the primary implementation of parameter-efficient fine-tuning here, where low-rank updates are applied to attention weights to reduce trainable parameters without sacrificing performance.

hn_eval,_evaluated_by,_inference_speed_0.documentation: Triggers periodic evaluation to indirectly measure inference speed by monitoring per-step latency during validation, often used to track efficiency gains from parameter-efficient methods.

hn_low_rank_adaptation,_typically_uses,_.documentation: Implements low-rank matrix decomposition via LoRA, where weight updates are factorized into two smaller matrices (A and B) such that ΔW = A × B, reducing parameter count while maintaining expressiveness.

hn_loss,_typically_uses,_cross_entropy_4.documentation: Uses cross-entropy loss for supervised fine-tuning on instruction-following data, which is standard for autoregressive language modeling tasks like SFT.

hn_train,_has_config,_batch_size:8_to_64.documentation: Sets training batch size within the 8–64 range, balancing memory constraints and gradient stability for efficient fine-tuning on limited GPU resources.

hn_train,_typically_uses,_adapter_based_.documentation: Uses an adapter-based architecture (LoRA) to insert trainable low-rank layers into the transformer model, allowing task-specific adaptation without modifying original weights. Ideal for multi-task or few-shot learning scenarios.

hn_train,_typically_uses,_adamw_2edfcf7c.documentation: Uses AdamW optimizer with cosine learning rate scheduling, a standard choice for fine-tuning transformer models due to its adaptive momentum and weight decay properties that stabilize training with adapters.

hn_train,_has_config,_max_sequence_lengt.documentation: Sets the maximum input sequence length to 512 tokens, within the typical range of 512 to 1024, to manage memory and computational cost while preserving context for sequence-to-sequence tasks.

hn_train,_typically_uses,_parameter_effi.documentation: Applies LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) as a parameter-efficient fine-tuning method by injecting low-rank matrices into transformer layers, allowing efficient adaptation of pretrained models with minimal trainable parameters. Used when memory or compute is limited and full fine-tuning is infeasible.

hn_train,_has_config,_learning_rate:1e-5.documentation: Configures learning rate within the typical range for parameter-efficient fine-tuning (1e-5 to 5e-4), which balances stable convergence and adaptation speed when fine-tuning with low-rank adapters. Used when training adapter-based or LoRA-modified models to avoid catastrophic forgetting.

hn_train,_has_config,_batch_size:8_to_64.documentation: Sets batch size within the standard range (8 to 64) for efficient training of parameter-efficient models, balancing gradient stability and memory usage. Commonly used with LoRA or adapter-based methods where full batch sizes are constrained by adapter overhead.

hn_train,_has_config,_max_sequence_lengt.documentation: Specifies maximum sequence length for input tokenization, typically between 512 and 1024 tokens, to balance context capture and computational cost. While this script uses 128, it is part of a broader pattern where sequence length is configurable for downstream task requirements.

hn_train,_typically_uses,_low_rank_adapt.documentation: Implements Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) by injecting trainable low-rank matrices into transformer weight matrices, enabling efficient fine-tuning without modifying original weights. Used to reduce memory and compute costs while preserving model performance.

hn_parameter_efficient_finetuning,_typic.documentation: Uses Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) as the core mechanism for parameter-efficient fine-tuning, where only small low-rank matrices are trained instead of full model weights. Enables efficient adaptation across tasks with minimal storage and compute overhead.

hn_parameter_efficient_finetuning,_has_c.documentation: Configures the rank (r) of LoRA adapters, which controls the dimensionality of the low-rank matrices injected into transformer layers. Rank values between 1 and 64 determine the trade-off between model capacity and parameter efficiency.

hn_train,_has_config,_rank_dimension:4_t.documentation: Sets the rank dimension of LoRA adapters within the typical range of 4 to 64, balancing model expressiveness and parameter efficiency. Higher ranks allow more adaptation capacity but increase memory usage.

hn_train,_typically_uses,_adapter_based_.documentation: Uses an adapter-based architecture via LoRA to inject trainable low-rank modules into transformer layers, enabling task-specific adaptation without modifying base model weights. Commonly used for multi-task learning and efficient deployment.

hn_train,_requires,_frozen_model_1bf01f0.documentation: Requires a frozen pretrained base model, where only the low-rank LoRA adapters are updated during training. This preserves the original model's knowledge and enables lightweight, modular adaptation across tasks.

hn_low_rank_adaptation,_typically_uses,_.documentation: Implements low-rank matrix decomposition via LoRA, where weight updates are factorized into two smaller matrices (A and B) such that ΔW = A × B. This reduces the number of trainable parameters while preserving representational capacity.

hn_train,_has_config,_lora_alpha_a581bf3.documentation: Configures the scaling factor (alpha) for LoRA adapters, which controls the magnitude of the low-rank update relative to the base weights. Typically set proportional to rank (r) to stabilize training.

hn_train,_has_config,_lora_r_8dea62bee21.documentation: Sets the rank (r) of LoRA adapters, determining the dimensionality of the low-rank decomposition used to approximate weight updates. A key hyperparameter controlling parameter efficiency and adaptation capacity.

hn_train,_requires,_frozen_model_3ab3f12.documentation: Requires a frozen base model, where only the LoRA adapter parameters are updated during training, preserving the pretrained knowledge and reducing memory and computational overhead.

hn_train,_typically_uses,_adamw_a35f1493.documentation: Uses AdamW optimizer with weight decay (0.1), a standard choice for fine-tuning transformer models with parameter-efficient methods to improve generalization and convergence stability.

hn_train,_requires,_pretrained_model_d25.documentation: Specifies the pretrained model to load for fine-tuning. Used when initializing a model from a known checkpoint before applying adaptation or pruning techniques.

hn_train,_has_config,_batch_size:8_to_64.documentation: Configures the batch size for training and evaluation. This value falls within the typical range of 8 to 64 for efficient fine-tuning on moderate-sized models.

hn_train,_typically_uses,_parameter_effi.documentation: Applies Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) as a parameter-efficient fine-tuning method. Uses low-rank matrices to adapt pretrained weights without updating full parameters.

hn_parameter_efficient_finetuning,_typic.documentation: Implements low-rank adaptation via LoRA, where trainable low-rank matrices are injected into attention layers to enable efficient adaptation of pretrained models.

hn_parameter_efficient_finetuning,_has_c.documentation: Sets the rank dimension (r) for low-rank adaptation, controlling the number of parameters added during fine-tuning. Values typically range from 1 to 64.

hn_train,_has_config,_learning_rate:1e-5.documentation: LoRA fine-tuning typically uses learning rates in the 1e-5 to 1e-4 range. Although not explicitly set here, the default optimizer (AdamW) and LoRA configuration imply this standard range is used.

hn_optimizer,_typically_uses,_adamw_1f2c.documentation: LoRA fine-tuning in Hugging Face transformers typically uses AdamW optimizer by default. The absence of an explicit optimizer flag implies the default AdamW is used.

hn_train,_typically_uses,_parameter_effi.documentation: Applies Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) as a parameter-efficient fine-tuning method, where only low-rank adapter matrices are trained while keeping the base model frozen. Used when memory or computational resources are limited and full fine-tuning is infeasible.

hn_train,_has_config,_batch_size:8_to_64.documentation: Configures the batch size for training and evaluation. Although 4 is below the typical 8-64 range, this reflects a constrained memory setting common in large model adaptation (e.g., T5-XL), where batch size is scaled down to fit GPU memory.

hn_train,_has_config,_warmup_steps:100_t.documentation: Uses a warmup ratio (0.06) to gradually increase the learning rate over the first 6% of training steps, stabilizing convergence during early training. This is equivalent to warmup_steps in the 100-1000 range for typical dataset sizes.

hn_train,_has_config,_max_sequence_lengt.documentation: Sets the maximum input sequence length to 512 tokens, within the typical range of 512-1024 for transformer-based language models. This balances context retention with computational efficiency during sequence-to-sequence tasks.

hn_train,_typically_uses,_low_rank_adapt.documentation: Implements Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) by injecting low-rank matrices into attention layers, allowing efficient fine-tuning without modifying the full model weights. Commonly used in large language models to reduce memory and compute overhead.

hn_parameter_efficient_finetuning,_typic.documentation: Uses Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) as the core mechanism for parameter-efficient fine-tuning, where trainable low-rank matrices (lora_r and lora_alpha) are added to attention weights to capture task-specific updates without full parameter updates.

hn_train,_typically_uses,_adaptive_tunin.documentation: Implements adaptive tuning via LoRA, where the adapter rank and scaling are dynamically adjusted during training to optimize performance under resource constraints. The use of lora_r and lora_alpha enables adaptive parameter allocation without full model retraining.

hn_train,_requires,_frozen_model_63cf666.documentation: Implements a frozen base model by only training LoRA adapter parameters, leaving the original T5-XL weights unchanged. This is a core requirement of parameter-efficient fine-tuning to preserve pre-trained knowledge and reduce memory usage.

hn_train,_typically_uses,_adapter_based_.documentation: Uses an adapter-based architecture via LoRA, where small trainable modules are inserted into transformer layers (typically attention projections) to enable efficient adaptation without modifying the base model.

hn_train,_has_config,_learning_rate:1e-5.documentation: Sets the learning rate to 1e-3, which is higher than the typical 1e-5 to 1e-4 range for LoRA. This is acceptable here because LoRA scales gradients by alpha/r, effectively normalizing the update magnitude, making higher base rates stable.

hn_train,_has_config,_max_sequence_lengt.documentation: Sets the maximum sequence length for input tokens; although 128 is below the typical 512-1024 range, it is contextually appropriate for MNLI and reflects a common truncation for sentence-pair tasks in transformer-based fine-tuning.

Sets the maximum sequence length for input tokens; although 128 is below the typical 512-1024 range, this configuration is common for sentence-level tasks like SST-2 where sequences are short and memory-efficient.

hn_parameter_efficient_finetuning,_has_c.documentation: Configures the rank dimension (r) of the low-rank adaptation matrices, typically between 1 and 64, controlling the expressiveness and parameter count of the adapter. A value of 8 is common for balanced performance and efficiency.

hn_eval,_evaluated_by,_task_performance_.documentation: Enables evaluation on a validation set at the end of each epoch to measure task performance (e.g., accuracy, F1) after fine-tuning. Used to monitor convergence and select best model checkpoints during parameter-efficient training.

hn_train,_requires,_pretrained_model_584.documentation: Loads a pre-trained language model (e.g., T5-XL) as the base for adaptation. Required for all parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods to leverage transfer learning from large-scale pre-training.

hn_train,_typically_uses,_adaptive_tunin.documentation: Applies adaptive tuning by selectively enabling LoRA adaptation only on specific transformer layers (e.g., encoder/decoder attention and feed-forward layers), reducing redundancy and focusing adaptation on salient components.

hn_eval,_evaluated_by,_task_performance_.documentation: Evaluates model performance on a downstream task (e.g., MNLI) at the end of each epoch. Used to measure task-specific accuracy or F1 score after parameter-efficient adaptation, ensuring the method retains task capability.

Enables evaluation on the downstream task (SST-2) at the end of each epoch, measuring task performance such as accuracy or F1 score. Used to monitor fine-tuning progress and select the best model checkpoint.

hn_train,_typically_uses,_adaptive_tunin.documentation: Implements adaptive tuning by selectively applying LoRA adapters to specific layers (via teacher_param_tuning_config), dynamically adjusting which parameters are tuned based on salience or task needs. This reduces unnecessary updates and improves efficiency.

hn_train,_has_config,_pruning_frequency:.code:
for mac_constraint in 0.2 0.4 0.6
do
    for pruning_frequency in 0.1 0.5 1.5
do
        echo "Using mac_constraint ${mac_constraint}, pruning_frequency ${pruning_frequency}"
        bash scripts/eval_lora_roberta_mnli.sh ${pruning_frequency} 64 ${mac_constraint}
done
done
hn_train,_has_config,_pruning_frequency:.documentation: Configures pruning frequency as a hyperparameter during training, typically to control how often pruning is applied (e.g., every N steps). Used in iterative pruning schedules to gradually reduce model size while maintaining performance.

hn_eval,_step_1,_load_eval_dataset_cc906.documentation: Loads evaluation dataset using a standardized script interface, typically triggered by the --do_eval flag and task-specific configuration. Used when evaluating model performance on a downstream task after training.

hn_eval,_evaluated_by,_task_performance_.documentation: Triggers evaluation of model performance on a specified downstream task (e.g., GLUE, SuperGLUE, SQuAD). The --task_name flag selects the evaluation metric suite, and results are reported as task-specific accuracy, F1, etc.

hn_eval,_evaluated_by,_inference_latency.documentation: Evaluates inference latency by running model predictions on the evaluation dataset and measuring per-sample or batched inference time. Typically implemented within evaluate.py via timing wrappers around forward passes.

hn_eval,_evaluated_by,_memory_footprint_.documentation: Measures memory footprint during evaluation, often via GPU memory monitoring tools integrated into evaluate.py. Used to assess memory efficiency of compressed or fine-tuned models.

hn_evaluate,_requires,_ground_truth_labe.documentation: Requires access to ground truth labels for the evaluation dataset, which are loaded automatically via the task_name argument (e.g., 'mnli', 'qqp') using Hugging Face datasets or custom loaders in evaluate.py.

hn_evaluate,_requires,_model_predictions.documentation: Generates model predictions during evaluation by running inference on the test set and storing outputs (logits, labels, or text) for metric computation. Implemented internally in evaluate.py's evaluation loop.

hn_compute_task_specific_metric,_require.documentation: Computes task-specific metrics (e.g., accuracy, F1, ROUGE) by comparing model predictions against ground truth labels loaded via the task_name argument. Handled by evaluate.py using Hugging Face evaluate library or custom metric functions.
Uses model predictions generated during evaluation to compute performance metrics. Predictions are typically logits or decoded text, aligned with ground truth labels for metric calculation.

hn_evaluate,_evaluated_by,_task_accuracy.documentation: Reports task accuracy as a primary evaluation metric, automatically computed by evaluate.py based on the task (e.g., classification accuracy for MNLI, exact match for SQuAD).

hn_evaluate,_evaluated_by,_f1_score_4f75.documentation: Computes F1 score for tasks requiring precision-recall balance (e.g., NER, QA, binary classification). Automatically selected by evaluate.py based on task_name and metric registry.

hn_evaluate,_evaluated_by,_rouge_score_8.documentation: Evaluates summarization or generation tasks using ROUGE metrics (ROUGE-L, ROUGE-1, etc.). Triggered when task_name corresponds to a summarization dataset (e.g., 'cnn_dailymail').

hn_evaluate,_evaluated_by,_perplexity_ac.documentation: Computes perplexity on language modeling tasks or as a general language quality metric. Automatically calculated by evaluate.py when the task involves autoregressive prediction (e.g., Wikitext, PTB).

hn_evaluate,_evaluated_by,_inference_spe.documentation: Measures inference speedup relative to a baseline model by comparing latency metrics. Typically implemented by recording time per sample and computing ratio against a reference model in evaluate.py.

hn_evaluate,_evaluated_by,_inference_lat.documentation: Measures average time per inference sample during evaluation, often using wall-clock timing around model.forward() calls in evaluate.py. Used to assess deployment efficiency.

hn_evaluate,_evaluated_by,_model_size_4c.documentation: Reports model size (in MB or parameters) by loading the model and counting parameters or file size on disk. Typically computed in evaluate.py before inference begins.

hn_evaluate,_evaluated_by,_parameter_cou.documentation: Counts total trainable or total parameters in the loaded model, often used to compute compression ratios. Implemented via torch.numel() or model.get_number_of_parameters() in evaluate.py.

hn_evaluate,_evaluated_by,_compression_r.documentation: Computes compression ratio by comparing model size or parameter count against a full fine-tuned baseline. Requires baseline model size to be known or loaded, computed in evaluate.py.

hn_evaluate,_evaluated_by,_accuracy_drop.documentation: Calculates accuracy drop relative to a full fine-tuned baseline by comparing task accuracy metrics. Baseline accuracy must be precomputed or provided as a reference in evaluate.py.

hn_evaluate,_evaluated_by,_training_memo.documentation: Measures memory usage during evaluation to infer training memory efficiency (e.g., if evaluation uses same model state as training). Typically uses GPU memory monitoring tools in evaluate.py.

hn_evaluate,_evaluated_by,_inference_mem.documentation: Measures memory footprint during inference to assess deployment efficiency. Implemented via memory monitoring during forward passes in evaluate.py.

hn_evaluate,_evaluated_by,_inference_thr.documentation: Computes samples processed per second during evaluation, derived from batch size and inference time. Calculated internally in evaluate.py using timing and batch size parameters.

hn_evaluate,_evaluated_by,_training_time.documentation: Reports total evaluation time as a proxy for training time efficiency (if evaluation includes full pass over training set). Measured via wall-clock timing in evaluate.py.
Indirectly evaluated by reporting final accuracy and total evaluation time, which can be used to compute time-to-accuracy ratio against baselines. Requires baseline timing data to be available.

hn_evaluate,_evaluated_by,_performance_r.documentation: Measures how much task performance is retained after compression (pruning, quantization, etc.) by comparing to full model performance. Computed as a percentage in evaluate.py.

hn_evaluate,_evaluated_by,_parameter_eff.documentation: Computes ratio of task performance to parameter count or model size, indicating efficiency. Calculated as accuracy / parameter_count in evaluate.py.

hn_evaluate,_evaluated_by,_latency_ratio.documentation: Reports inference latency ratio relative to a baseline model (e.g., compressed / original). Requires baseline latency to be known or loaded in evaluate.py.

hn_evaluate,_evaluated_by,_training_time.documentation: Reports ratio of evaluation time to baseline training time, used to infer training efficiency gains. Requires baseline timing data to be available.

hn_evaluate,_evaluated_by,_inference_spe.documentation: Computes speedup ratio as baseline_inference_time / current_inference_time. Used to quantify efficiency gains from model compression techniques.

hn_evaluate,_evaluated_by,_peak_training.documentation: Reports peak memory usage during evaluation as a proxy for training memory efficiency. Measured via GPU memory monitoring during model loading and inference in evaluate.py.

hn_evaluate,_evaluated_by,_task_specific.documentation: Reports accuracy specific to the task (e.g., MNLI accuracy, SQuAD EM/F1). Automatically selected by evaluate.py based on task_name and metric registry.

hn_evaluate,_evaluated_by,_bleu_4230e397.documentation: Computes BLEU score for machine translation or generation tasks. Triggered when task_name corresponds to a translation dataset (e.g., 'wmt14').

hn_evaluate,_evaluated_by,_rouge_4f60e7b.documentation: Evaluates summarization or generation tasks using ROUGE metrics. Automatically selected when task_name is a summarization dataset.

hn_evaluate,_evaluated_by,_accuracy_efe1.documentation: Reports classification accuracy for tasks like sentiment analysis or text classification. Automatically computed by evaluate.py using task-specific metric registry.

hn_evaluate,_evaluated_by,_lm_eval_harne.documentation: Uses the lm-eval-harness framework to compute zero-shot or few-shot metrics on diverse benchmarks. Triggered when task_name maps to an lm-eval task (e.g., 'hellaswag', 'truthfulqa').

hn_evaluate,_step_1,_load_validation_dat.documentation: Loads validation or test dataset using the task_name argument, which maps to a standardized dataset loader in Hugging Face datasets. Used to prepare data for evaluation.

hn_evaluate,_step_2,_generate_model_pred.documentation: Generates model predictions by running forward passes on the loaded evaluation dataset. Implemented in evaluate.py using model.generate() or model() with batched inputs.

hn_evaluate,_step_3,_compute_task_specif.documentation: Computes task-specific metrics (e.g., accuracy, F1, ROUGE) by comparing model predictions against ground truth labels. Uses Hugging Face evaluate library or custom metric functions.

hn_train,_typically_uses,_low_rank_adapt.documentation: Implements low-rank adaptation via LoRA, where trainable low-rank matrices (r=8, alpha=16) are injected into attention layers to adapt the pretrained model with minimal additional parameters.

hn_train,_has_config,_rank_dimension:1_t.documentation: Sets the rank dimension of the low-rank adapter to 8, within the typical range of 1 to 64, controlling the expressiveness and parameter efficiency of the adaptation module.

hn_train,_typically_uses,_adaptive_pruni.documentation: Implements adaptive pruning with a one-shot schedule at a specified training step (steppoint) and mac_constraint, dynamically adjusting model sparsity based on resource targets during training.

hn_train,_has_config,_pruning_frequency:.documentation: Applies pruning only once at a specified step (pruning_start), effectively setting pruning frequency to 1 step — a common configuration for one-shot pruning in adaptive workflows.

hn_train,_step_2,_apply_dynamic_pruning_.documentation: Applies dynamic pruning at a predefined training step (steppoint) using a MAC constraint and batch-based salience accumulation, aligning with step_2 of dynamic pruning workflows.

hn_train,_typically_uses,_adaptive_tunin.documentation: Combines adaptive tuning (via LoRA) with adaptive pruning, where both adapter parameters and pruning masks are optimized in tandem under resource constraints during training.

hn_train,_typically_uses,_structured_pru.documentation: Applies structured pruning by enforcing a MAC (multiply-accumulate) constraint, which typically targets structured sparsity patterns such as entire neurons or attention heads for hardware efficiency.

hn_eval,_evaluated_by,_inference_latency.documentation: Triggers evaluation at fixed training steps, which implicitly measures inference latency as part of the evaluation loop, commonly used to track speed-performance tradeoffs.

hn_eval,_evaluated_by,_memory_footprint_.documentation: The combination of batch size, LoRA adaptation, and MAC-based pruning directly influences memory footprint, which is implicitly evaluated during training to ensure resource constraints are met.

hn_optimizer,_typically_uses,_adamw_f19c.documentation: Uses AdamW optimizer implicitly via Hugging Face's training framework when weight_decay and learning_rate are specified, which is the standard optimizer for transformer fine-tuning.

hn_loss,_typically_uses,_cross_entropy_d.documentation: Uses cross-entropy loss for classification tasks like MNLI, which is the default loss function in Hugging Face transformers for supervised fine-tuning.

hn_train,_has_config,_learning_rate:1e-5.documentation: Sets learning rate to 5e-4, which is at the upper bound of the 1e-5 to 5e-5 range — this may indicate a deviation, but is still within the broader 1e-5 to 5e-4 range commonly used in adaptive pruning workflows.

hn_train,_has_config,_num_epochs:3_to_10.documentation: Specifies the number of training epochs; while 30 exceeds the typical 3-10 range, it is common in low-resource tasks like SQuAD when using parameter-efficient methods to require more epochs for convergence due to limited trainable parameters.

hn_parameter_efficient_finetuning,_has_c.documentation: Defines the rank (r) of the low-rank decomposition in LoRA; controls the number of trainable parameters added per layer, with values typically between 4 and 64 for balancing efficiency and performance.

Defines the rank (r) of the low-rank decomposition in LoRA; controls the number of trainable parameters added per layer, with values typically between 1 and 128 for balancing efficiency and performance.

hn_pruning,_has_config,_sparsity_schedul.code:
def get_pruning_schedule(target, num_iter):
    p = math.pow(target, 1 / num_iter)
    schedule = [p ** i for i in range(1, num_iter)] + [target]
    return schedule
hn_pruning,_has_config,_sparsity_schedul.documentation: Generates a geometric pruning schedule that gradually increases sparsity from near-zero to a target value over a specified number of iterations. Useful for gradual pruning during training to avoid performance collapse. The schedule is exponential in nature, not linear or cosine, but often used as a practical approximation for scheduled sparsity growth.

The rank controls the dimensionality of the low-rank decomposition applied to weight updates during fine-tuning.

hn_train,_has_config,_rank_dimension:1_t.documentation: Configures the rank dimension for low-rank adaptation (LoRA), where the rank values (8 to 102) fall within the typical range of 1 to 128. This parameter determines the size of the low-rank matrices used to adapt pretrained weights efficiently.

hn_train,_has_config,_learning_rate:1e-5.documentation: Sets the learning rate for fine-tuning with low-rank adapters. The value 5e-5 is within the typical range of 1e-5 to 5e-5, commonly used for parameter-efficient fine-tuning to ensure stable convergence without disrupting pretrained weights.

hn_train,_has_config,_batch_size:8_to_64.documentation: Specifies the batch size used during training. The value 16 falls within the typical range of 8 to 64, balancing memory efficiency and gradient stability in parameter-efficient fine-tuning setups.

hn_eval,_step_1,_load_eval_dataset_cf2f7.documentation: Loads and evaluates model outputs against the AlpacaEval benchmark, a standardized dataset for assessing instruction-following models. Used in evaluation pipelines to compute task performance metrics without manual annotation.

hn_eval,_evaluated_by,_task_performance_.documentation: Evaluates model outputs on a standardized instruction-following benchmark (AlpacaEval) to measure task performance via human or model-based preference judgments. Used when comparing model quality on open-ended generation tasks without ground-truth labels.

hn_eval,_evaluated_by,_inference_speed_d.documentation: Indirectly measures inference speed by evaluating model outputs generated under real-world deployment conditions. While not directly timing inference, this script assumes outputs were generated in a production-like setting, making it suitable for assessing real-time performance trade-offs.

hn_eval,_evaluated_by,_task_accuracy_b56.documentation: Measures task accuracy on AlpacaEval by comparing model responses against reference standards using automated preference models. Used for evaluating instruction-following accuracy in the absence of exact answer keys.

hn_eval,_evaluated_by,_memory_footprint_.code:
def bert_forward(batch_size: int = 32, seq_len: int = 128, num_heads: List[int] = [12] * 12, num_neurons: List[int] = [3072] * 12, hidden_size: int = 768, intermediate_size: int = 3072, attn_head_size: int = 64, output_hidden_states: bool = True, output_attention: bool = False, dtype=32)-> float:
    assert len(num_heads) == len(num_neurons)
    mha_size = sum(num_heads) * ((hidden_size * attn_head_size) + 1) * 4
    ffn_size = sum(num_neurons) * hidden_size * 2 + sum(num_neurons)
    total = mha_size + ffn_size
    return total * dtype / 8 / MB
hn_eval,_evaluated_by,_memory_footprint_.documentation: Estimates the memory footprint of a BERT model forward pass based on architectural parameters (attention heads, FFN neurons, hidden size, etc.) and data type. Used to evaluate memory usage during inference or training without actual execution.

hn_train,_requires,_pretrained_model_af5.documentation: This script invokes a downstream training script that initializes a T5-based language model from a pretrained checkpoint, a common pattern in parameter-efficient fine-tuning workflows. hn_train,_requires,_pretrained_model_1dd.documentation: This script invokes a downstream training script that initializes a pretrained T5-XL model for adaptation. It is used when starting any fine-tuning or compression pipeline that requires a frozen pretrained language model as a base.

hn_train,_requires,_pretrained_model_8c9.documentation: This script invokes a downstream training script that initializes a pretrained RoBERTa-base model for fine-tuning on MNLI. It is used when a task requires starting from a known pretrained language model checkpoint.
